description: David Hume's scepticism about causation — his argument that we never observe necessary connection between events, only constant conjunction — famously 'woke Kant from his dogmatic slumber' (Kant's own phrase). Hume had argued that the concept of causation cannot be derived from experience; Kant accepted this challenge but reversed its conclusion: if causation cannot come from experience, it must be a contribution of the mind itself. The entire architecture of the Critique of Pure Reason is shaped by Kant's attempt to answer Hume without retreating into dogmatic rationalism.
source: Kant, Prolegomena to Any Future Metaphysics (1783), Preface; also Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748)
